PB notes: This crazy person/bot again with their insane RTs.
RC notes: Does seem crazy that their mean go rt is -2.59 standard deviations below the mean across subjects (649 ms), but I don’t think this is biologically implausible. See go nogo notes on this subject for more information on their bot-like-ness, but I’m inclined to accept that they’re just on the extreme end of the rt distribution, and include – MM

PB notes: waited for the stop signal. We need a threshold for what is too much waiting
RC notes: Waited for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in every session. Exclude – MM

PB notes: didn't stop at all. This subject is an candiate for exclusion
RC notes: Never learned to stop in practice; maybe the failed-stop trial-level feedback (“There was a star”)  was not informative enough to make them realize their misunderstanding, but I don't think its something we have to worry about changing since it seems like just this subject. Think we have to exclude this session – MM

flipped_mappings: block 1 (85.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block); block 2 (100.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block); block 3 (90.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block)
PB notes: something went really wrong in sessions 2-3. Did they mix up the mapping?
RC notes: Gets the first 3 go trials correct in the first block, then must have flipped the mappings because 0% go choice accuracy for the rest of the block and the second block, and 5% choice accuracy in the last block. If we flip mappings for them, we could include this session – MM

flipped_mappings: block 1 (100.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block); block 2 (100.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block); block 3 (100.0% improvement in choice accuracy that block)

PB notes: waited for the stop signal in most sessions, but seemingly not too severelye
RC notes: Waited for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in nearly every session. They do get a little better, but probably have to exclude – MM

PB notes: why did this person get so much feedback? Slow RT?
RC notes: Yes – the threshold for slow response feedback is 750 ms. In this first session, they got slow rt feedback twice and also got omission feedback (threshold is >10%) after the second block. Looks like waiting for stop (go rt > 750 ms). Exclude – MM

PB notes: omitted most responses in ses 3. Clustered? Candidate for session-level exclusion
RC notes: ~75% of go omissions are clustered. Omitted roughly the same proportion of go trials each block (63%-75%). The longest string of trials with no responses was 12 in the last block. We should probably exclude this session (omissions >50%). – MM

PB notes: decent amount of omissions. Clustered?
RC notes: Majority (87%) of omissions interspersed. Didn’t respond for 5 consecutive trials in the first block, but only ⅖ were go trials. Stable omission rate across blocks (13-15%). In the last block, all go omissions were preceded by stop trials (67% successful), but in the first two blocks the majority of go omissions were preceded by successful go trials. Not sure why so bad here, but did have mildly worse omission rates in other tasks this session. No sessions bad enough to exclude – MM

PB notes: pretty slow in this session and generally. Need a threshold for too much slowing
RC notes: Waited too long (go rt > 750 ms) in sessions 3-5. Maybe exclude entirely – MM

PB notes: this person has really bad go accuracy and it isn't their first session. Worth looking at their trial data.
RC notes: No clues in trial data or feedback as to why they did so much worse this session. High go accuracy in practice. First block was the worst (63% choice accuracy and 4 go omissions, interspersed) and then go accuracy was roughly stable for second and third blocks (73% and 75%), with no go omissions. Mildly more omissions in two other tasks this session, so maybe having an off day. Think we should still include – MM

PB notes: why such high feedback?
RC notes: They got two types of feedback after their first block: responding too slowly (threshold: 750 ms), and slowing down to wait for the star (threshold: stop-failure rate <25%). After their second block, they got feedback for not stopping when the star is present (threshold: stop-failure rate >75%). – MM

PB notes: clustered omissions? Might need to exclude this session.
RC notes: Yes, 100% are clustered in two places. Responded to every go trial in the first block. In second and third blocks, omitted the last 38 and 57 trials of each block, respectively. Think we have to exclude session 2 – MM

PB notes: horrendous performance in all sessions. Probably need to exclude all of their stopping data
RC notes: Yep, this person was awful in every task. Exclude entirely – MM

PB notes: lots of omissions. Clustered?
RC notes: 91% of omissions were clustered, mostly in the second block. There was a cluster of 6 in the first block and 4 interspersed (which seems weird, but not too terrible). The rest of go omissions occurred in the second block, when they didn’t respond to the first 32 trials (also didn’t respond to attention check before). Include session, and flag the large cluster – MM

PB comments: is something wrong with the separation of subjects here?

PB notes: waited for the stop signal. We need a threshold for what is too much waiting
RC notes: Waited for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in every session. Exclude – MM

PB notes: seemed to basically just omit all ses 5 responses. Exclude ses 5
RC notes: Agreed, this is wayyy above our 50% omission threshold. Exclude this session – MM

PB notes: waited for the stop signal, especially in latter sessions. We need a threshold for what is too much waiting
RC notes: Waited for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in every session. Exclude – MM

PB notes: this is bad go accuracy. Just normal choice errors only in session 2?
RC notes: Looks like they flipped mappings in the first block. Only 15% choice accuracy. In the other two blocks, choice accuracy jumped to 90% and 98%. We could flip mappings for them. Include  – MM
PB comments: will you be rectifying this flipped mapping?

PB notes: decent omissions in ses 4 and 5. Clustered?
RC notes: Mostly (80%) interspersed. In the first two blocks, 1 go omitted in each. In the last block, omitted a total of 13 go trials. The longest sequence of trials with no responses was 6 in a row (3 of which were go trials). Kinda bad, but not bad enough to exclude. – MM

All interspersed, except for one cluster of 3 in the second block. Go omission rate is 10% for the first block, 30% for the second block, and 5% for the third block. Strange, but not bad enough to exclude (less than <25%)  – MM

PB notes: decent omissions in ses  5. Clustered?
RC notes: Not clustered. Longest string of trials with no responses is 4 (3 of which are stops). 20% go omitted in the first block, 10% in the second block, and 12.5% in the last block. Not great, but still include – MM

PB notes: waited for the stop signal. We need a threshold for what is too much waiting
RC notes: Waited too long for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in 3 sessions and just barely under threshold in other 2. Probably exclude all sessions – MM

PB notes: waited for the stop signal. We need a threshold for what is too much waiting
RC notes: Waited too long for stop (go rt > 750 ms) in 3 sessions and pretty slow in other 2. Probably exclude all sessions – MM
